title: Coconut ice
subtitle: No-bake confection of desiccated coconut and condensed milk in pink and white layers
excerpt: A no-cook confection of desiccated coconut, condensed milk, and icing sugar pressed into layers: one white, one pink. It sets firm enough to cut into squares without baking or cooking. The sweetness comes from the condensed milk; the icing sugar thickens the mixture to a mouldable consistency. Associated with school fairs and Christmas.
sourceNotes: Coconut ice as a confection appears in British cookbooks and household guides from the late 19th century, when desiccated coconut became widely imported and available in British grocery stores. The combination of condensed milk and desiccated coconut was a standard cheap sweet in British cooking from the early 20th century. The pink-and-white two-layer presentation became the conventional form associated with school fundraisers and Christmas gift hampers. The recipe requires no cooking, which made it accessible for children and domestic cooks without specialist equipment.

content.text: Coconut ice needs no cooking and no equipment beyond a bowl and a tin. The

happens at room temperature over 2 hours. The mixture should be pressed firmly into the tin so the layers compact together; loosely packed coconut ice crumbles when cut. If you want a deeper pink, use gel food colouring rather than liquid, which can make the mixture too wet.

attrs.prepNote: pink or red gel food colouring

content.text: Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. In a large bowl, mix the sifted icing sugar and desiccated coconut together. Add the condensed milk and stir until the mixture forms a stiff dough that holds its shape. If it is too crumbly, add condensed milk a teaspoon at a time; if too wet, add icing sugar.

Divide the mixture in half. Press the white half firmly into the base of the prepared tin, spreading it evenly to 1.5 cm depth. Mix the pink food colouring into the remaining half and knead until the colour is even. Press the pink layer firmly on top of the white layer. Cover and leave to set at room temperature for at least 2 hours. Lift out by the paper, place on a board, and cut into squares with a sharp knife. Store in layers separated by baking paper.
